Gene_Accession_Number.reason: The paper identifies the gene as Glyma.11g108300/Glyma.11G108300 in the Chr. 11 GWAS locus.

Species.reason: The study is on soybean and repeatedly names soybean as the species tested.

Species_Latin_Name.reason: The title/abstract explicitly gives soybean as Glycine max (L.) Merr.

Origin_Species.reason: The gene characterized is from soybean.

Origin_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Soybean is explicitly identified as Glycine max.

Origin_Species_Variety.reason: The coding sequence used for functional work is from W82, and all soybean lines are stated to be in the Williams 82 background unless otherwise noted.

Variety.reason: W82 is the main transgenic background, and Kefeng-1 was used specifically for the hairy root overexpression assay.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: The paper states it is a cytochrome P450, called CYP76F17, belonging to the CYP76 subfamily.

Enzyme_Name.reason: The authors explicitly redesignate GmCYP76F17 as GmIF6H1, isoflavone 6-hydroxylase 1.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: Results and discussion place GmIF6H1 in isoflavonoid biosynthesis and specifically glycitein/glycitein-type isoflavonoid biosynthesis.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: The paper shows GmIF6H1 converts daidzein to 6-hydroxydaidzein for glycitein production, i.e. the daidzein-to-glycitein 6-hydroxylation branch.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: The study presents GmIF6H1 as a downstream tailoring step acting on daidzein and as a branch competing with glyceollin formation from the same precursor.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: Yeast, microsomal assays, and isotope tracing support C6 hydroxylation of daidzein to 6-hydroxydaidzein; yeast assays also show activity toward formononetin and biochanin A.

Primary_Substrate.reason: The paper explicitly concludes that daidzein is the bona fide substrate in planta.

Primary_Product.reason: The enzymatic product from daidzein is identified as 6-hydroxydaidzein.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: The paper shows GmIF6H1 forms the immediate precursor to glycitein and that overexpression reduces glyceollin I during infection, indicating flux diversion.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The manuscript explicitly calls GmIF6H1 a key determinant and shows dosage-sensitive effects on defense balance; it does not claim strict rate limitation, matching the cautious wording here.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: CRISPR mutants nearly lose glycitein-type isoflavonoids; overexpression elevates 6-hydroxydaidzein derivatives and alters defense metabolite partitioning with reduced glyceollin I during infection.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: The paper connects GmIF6H1 to glycitein and glycitein-type isoflavonoids.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Glycitein is an isoflavonoid and is discussed as part of glycitein-type isoflavonoids.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Results support a defensive role against P. sojae; the broader statement that isoflavonoids benefit human health appears in introduction and discussion, so the first part is directly supported and the second is general background stated in the paper.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Fig. 5 states glycitein-type compounds preferentially accumulate in mature roots and hypocotyls; seeds were profiled in the natural population.

Expression_Pattern.reason: The paper states GmIF6H1 expression mirrors glycitein accumulation, being preferential in mature roots and hypocotyls, and remains unchanged upon P. sojae infection.

Expression_Pattern_of_Origin_Species.reason: Preferential expression in mature roots and hypocotyls is directly reported.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: GmCYP76F17-GFP colocalized with an ER marker.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: All listed treatments/contexts appear in Results or Methods: P. sojae infection, daidzein-d6 feeding, germinating hypocotyls, yeast feeding, and ion/pH/temperature assays.

Promoters.reason: The paper explicitly describes CaMV 35S promoter-driven GmIF6H1 transgenic lines.

Omics_Data.reason: The study uses metabolome GWAS on 727 accessions with LC-qToF-MS, transcriptome profiling of inoculated hypocotyls, and prior resequencing/SNP data including 53 soja accessions.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Results directly show both knockout and overexpression increase susceptibility to P. sojae, overexpression lowers glyceollin I, and the domestication-associated haplotype has lower activity/glycitein-type metabolites.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Each point is present: former name GmCYP76F17, in vitro higher activity toward formononetin/biochanin A, daidzein established in planta, T248A polymorphism effects, trace glycitein in mutants after infection, and metabolon idea proposed but not proven.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Most listed methods are directly used (mGWAS, hairy root OE, yeast assay, microsomal kinetics, localization, CRISPR, isotope tracing, LC-MS, pathogen assay), but the chemical complementation description names glycitein here whereas the Results and Methods report exogenous glycinein/glycitein inconsistently. Because of that mismatch, this field is only partially secure.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper explicitly discusses opportunities for engineering disease-resilient soybean and the need for precise tuning of GmIF6H1; haplotype effects at residue 248 support breeding relevance.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: The summary matches the Results: GWAS identification, daidzein C6-hydroxylation to 6-hydroxydaidzein, knockout phenotype, overexpression effects, susceptibility of both LOF and OE, and T248A/haplotype effects.

Species.reason: The gene is discussed in soybean defense metabolism context.

Species_Latin_Name.reason: The study species is Glycine max.

Origin_Species.reason: GmI2'H is referenced as a soybean pathway gene.

Origin_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The soybean species is Glycine max.

Variety.reason: The pathway model and infection experiments use W82 background, but GmI2'H itself was not directly tested in a specific W82 assay here.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: The paper refers to glyceollins being synthesized via daidzein 2'-hydroxylation and names GmI2'H in Discussion/Fig. 6G, but does not explicitly characterize its protein family/domain in this paper.

Enzyme_Name.reason: The gene symbol GmI2'H denotes/is discussed as isoflavone 2'-hydroxylase in the defense model.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: The paper places GmI2'H in isoflavonoid/glyceollin biosynthesis in the defense scheme.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: Fig. 6G and text explicitly describe glyceollins as arising from the daidzein 2'-hydroxylation branch.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: The manuscript proposes daidzein partitioning between GmIF6H1 and GmI2'H branches.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: The paper states glyceollins are produced via daidzein 2'-hydroxylation and Fig. 6G assigns this branch to GmI2'H.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Daidzein is explicitly described as the shared precursor for glycitein and glyceollin I.

Primary_Product.reason: The exact immediate product is not named in Results; only 2'-hydroxylation-derived intermediates for glyceollin biosynthesis are inferable.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: The paper clearly links the GmI2'H branch to glyceollin production.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: This is an inferential model from metabolite changes in GmIF6H1 lines; GmI2'H itself was not manipulated or directly assayed in this paper.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Glyceollin I/glyceollins are explicitly the end products of this branch in the paper.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Glyceollins are explicitly described as phytoalexins, i.e. isoflavonoid phytoalexins.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper states glyceollin I is a major phytoalexin inhibitory to P. sojae and glyceollins act as inducible phytoalexins.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Glyceollin I was quantified in inoculated hypocotyls during P. sojae infection.

Expression_Pattern.reason: The paper discusses infection-induced expression of isoflavonoid biosynthesis genes generally, but does not clearly present a specific GmI2'H expression pattern field in the provided Results text.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: The relevant context for this branch is P. sojae infection, explicitly studied.

Omics_Data.reason: Transcriptome profiling of inoculated hypocotyls is reported for isoflavonoid-pathway genes, but no direct omics result for GmI2'H is clearly shown in the provided text.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: The proposed competition for the daidzein pool between GmIF6H1 and GmI2'H is central to Fig. 6G and discussion.

Other_Important_Info.reason: This wording accurately states that GmI2'H is included in the model/discussion but not directly manipulated, and that the perturbation mechanism remains unresolved.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Only indirect support exists via infection metabolite profiling and pathway inference; there is no direct functional validation of GmI2'H in this paper.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: This is a reasonable inference from the paper's defense model, but explicit engineering recommendations for GmI2'H are not directly made.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: The summary accurately reflects that GmI2'H is presented as the glyceollin branch enzyme and that flux competition with GmIF6H1 is proposed, while noting it was not directly tested.
